name: Media Psychology
description: An examination of the psychological processes underlying media consumption, media effects on cognition, emotion, identity, and behavior, and media literacy as a protective skill.

modules.title: Introduction to Media Psychology
modules.objectives: Define media psychology as a field, trace its history, identify major research questions, and distinguish psychological from sociological and communication approaches to media.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 1 — Introduction to Psychology
modules.assignment: Keep a 24-hour media diary documenting every media interaction (screen time, music, podcasts, social media). Write a 300-word analysis of patterns you notice and three questions a media psychologist might ask about your media behavior.

modules.title: Theories of Media Effects: Cultivation & Agenda-Setting
modules.titleZh: 媒体效果理论：涵化与议程设置
modules.objectives: Explain cultivation theory (Gerbner) and its predictions about heavy TV viewing, describe agenda-setting theory (McCombs & Shaw), and evaluate evidence for and against each theory.
modules.assignment: Design a mini survey (10 questions) testing a cultivation theory hypothesis (e.g., heavy crime-drama viewers overestimate crime rates). Administer it to five people, tabulate results, and write a 350-word analysis of findings in relation to Gerbner's predictions.

modules.title: Attention & Digital Media
modules.objectives: Explain models of attention (spotlight, filtering), analyze how digital media design exploits attentional mechanisms, and evaluate research on media multitasking and cognitive load.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Smartphones Hijack Your Attention
modules.assignment: Conduct a self-experiment: spend one hour studying with your phone face-up and one hour with phone hidden. Record focus quality and tasks completed each hour. Write a 350-word analysis connecting your results to research on media multitasking and cognitive load.

modules.title: Persuasion & Advertising
modules.objectives: Explain the Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM) of persuasion, analyze persuasion techniques in advertising (authority, social proof, scarcity, emotional appeals), and evaluate their psychological mechanisms.
modules.assignment: Collect three advertisements (print, video, or social media). For each, identify the ELM route used (central or peripheral), the specific persuasion techniques employed, and the target audience. Write a 400-word comparative analysis.

modules.title: Media Violence & Aggression
modules.objectives: Summarize research on media violence and aggression (General Aggression Model), evaluate methodological limitations of experimental and longitudinal studies, and critically assess policy implications.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Does Media Violence Cause Aggression?
modules.assignment: Write a 450-word evidence-based essay evaluating whether violent video games cause real-world aggression. Include the strongest argument for a causal link, the strongest argument against, and your own conclusion supported by specific research findings.

modules.title: Social Media & Self-Concept
modules.objectives: Explain how social comparison theory applies to social media use, analyze research on social media and self-esteem, loneliness, and well-being, and describe identity curation on digital platforms.
modules.assignment: Audit your own social media profile. Write a 400-word analysis of how your profile presents a 'possible self': what aspects of yourself you emphasize, what you omit, and how this compares to Goffman's concept of self-presentation and impression management.

modules.title: Online Identity & Avatars
modules.objectives: Analyze how online anonymity, pseudonymity, and avatar customization affect identity expression and behavior (Proteus Effect), and evaluate the psychological implications of online identity.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Psychology of Online Identity
modules.assignment: Design an avatar for a fictional online game character and a professional LinkedIn profile for the same fictional person. Write a 400-word reflection contrasting how each platform shapes identity expression differently, applying the Proteus Effect and Goffman's dramaturgical model.

modules.title: Gaming & Virtual Reality
modules.objectives: Evaluate research on the psychological effects of video gaming (positive: problem-solving, prosocial; negative: addiction risk, aggression), and analyze emerging VR technology applications in mental health.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Reinforcement & Learning in Games
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Psychology of Video Games
modules.assignment: Analyze a video game you have played or are familiar with. Write a 400-word psychological analysis identifying: the operant conditioning schedules used to maintain play, three positive psychological skills the game develops, and two potential risks for vulnerable players.

modules.title: News Media & Political Attitudes
modules.objectives: Explain how selective exposure, confirmation bias, and filter bubbles shape political attitudes, analyze the psychological impact of partisan news framing, and evaluate misinformation research.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Filter Bubbles Isolate You
modules.assignment: Compare how the same news event is covered by two news outlets with contrasting political leanings. Write a 400-word analysis identifying framing differences, the emotional language used, what facts are emphasized or omitted, and how each framing might influence audience attitudes.

modules.title: Media & Body Image
modules.objectives: Analyze how media images influence body image and eating attitudes using social comparison and objectification theory, evaluate research on thin-ideal media and disordered eating, and propose media literacy interventions.
modules.assignment: Analyze 10 images from a mainstream fashion or fitness magazine or social media account. Write a 400-word analysis applying objectification theory and social comparison theory: what body ideals are presented, who is represented, and what psychological impact this may have on viewers.

modules.title: Children & Media
modules.objectives: Evaluate research on media's effects on child cognitive and social development, describe age-based recommendations from the American Academy of Pediatrics, and analyze prosocial media effects in children.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Screen Time and Child Development
modules.assignment: Write a 400-word evidence-based parent guide for managing a child's media use (ages 3–10). Include the AAP guidelines, three specific media choices that support development, and two parent strategies for healthy media habits.

quizQuestions.question: Media psychology primarily studies:
options: How to design better media products
The relationship between media use and psychological processes and outcomes
quizQuestions.answer: The relationship between media use and psychological processes and outcomes
quizQuestions.explanation: Media psychology examines how people use, respond to, and are affected by media — using psychological theory and empirical methods.

quizQuestions.question: The average American adult spends approximately how many hours per day consuming media?
quizQuestions.answer: 7–12 hours
quizQuestions.explanation: Nielsen and other research bodies consistently estimate US adult media consumption at 7–12 hours daily across all screens and audio.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Media psychology primarily studies:" is: The relationship between media use and psychological processes and outcomes.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The relationship between media use and psychological processes and outcomes is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Cultivation theory (Gerbner) predicts that heavy television viewers will:
options: Have more accurate perceptions of reality than light viewers
Overestimate the prevalence of violence and crime in the real world
Consume more news than light viewers
quizQuestions.answer: Overestimate the prevalence of violence and crime in the real world
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultivation theory holds that heavy TV viewing creates a distorted, 'mean world' view because TV overrepresents crime and danger.

quizQuestions.question: Agenda-setting theory (McCombs & Shaw) argues that news media:
options: Tell audiences what to think about by emphasizing certain issues
Have no effect on public priorities
quizQuestions.answer: Tell audiences what to think about by emphasizing certain issues
quizQuestions.explanation: Agenda-setting theory holds that media don't tell people what to think but what issues to think about — shaping public salience of topics.

quizQuestions.question: The 'mean world syndrome' described by Gerbner refers to:
options: Belief that the world is more violent and dangerous than it actually is
quizQuestions.answer: Belief that the world is more violent and dangerous than it actually is
quizQuestions.explanation: Mean world syndrome is the cultivation effect whereby heavy TV viewers, exposed to much fictional violence, come to believe the world is a more dangerous place.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Cultivation theory (Gerbner) predicts that heavy television viewers will:" is: Overestimate the prevalence of violence and crime in the real world.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Overestimate the prevalence of violence and crime in the real world is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Media multitasking research consistently shows that heavy media multitaskers:
options: Are better at filtering irrelevant information
Show poorer performance on sustained attention tasks
quizQuestions.answer: Show poorer performance on sustained attention tasks
quizQuestions.explanation: Ophir et al. found heavy media multitaskers performed worse at filtering distractors and switching tasks — counter to the belief that multitasking improves cognitive flexibility.

quizQuestions.question: Infinite scroll and notification systems on social media platforms are designed to exploit:
options: Variable reinforcement schedules and attentional capture
quizQuestions.answer: Variable reinforcement schedules and attentional capture
quizQuestions.explanation: These design features use variable rewards (intermittent interesting content) and involuntary attentional capture to maximize engagement and time on platform.

quizQuestions.question: The 'attentional economy' refers to:
options: The competition for limited human attention as a scarce resource by media companies
Financial markets focusing on technology stocks
quizQuestions.answer: The competition for limited human attention as a scarce resource by media companies
quizQuestions.explanation: The attentional economy conceptualizes human attention as a finite resource that digital platforms compete to capture and monetize.

quizQuestions.question: The Elaboration Likelihood Model's 'central route' to persuasion involves:
options: Superficial processing of peripheral cues like attractiveness
Careful consideration of the quality of the message's arguments
quizQuestions.answer: Careful consideration of the quality of the message's arguments
quizQuestions.explanation: The central route involves high elaboration — thoughtful evaluation of argument quality — and produces durable, resistant attitude change.

quizQuestions.question: The peripheral route to persuasion (ELM) is most likely to operate when:
options: The issue is personally relevant and important
Motivation and ability to process are high
Motivation or ability to process the message is low
quizQuestions.answer: Motivation or ability to process the message is low
quizQuestions.explanation: The peripheral route relies on heuristics and surface cues (celebrity endorser, attractive packaging) when people lack motivation or ability to analyze arguments.

quizQuestions.question: Fear appeals in health advertising are most effective when they:
options: Maximize fear with no solution offered
Pair high-threat information with credible, specific, achievable recommended actions (high efficacy)
Target people who are already healthy
quizQuestions.answer: Pair high-threat information with credible, specific, achievable recommended actions (high efficacy)
quizQuestions.explanation: Extended Parallel Process Model: fear appeals work when fear is high AND perceived efficacy of the recommended action is also high.

quizQuestions.question: Product placement in media works as a persuasion technique primarily through:
quizQuestions.answer: Classical conditioning and repeated exposure
quizQuestions.explanation: Product placement works by associating products with liked characters/situations (classical conditioning) and through the mere exposure effect.

quizQuestions.question: Anderson and Bushman's General Aggression Model proposes that media violence increases aggression by:
options: Teaching aggression as a social norm, increasing aggressive thoughts, and priming hostile interpretations
quizQuestions.answer: Teaching aggression as a social norm, increasing aggressive thoughts, and priming hostile interpretations
quizQuestions.explanation: The GAM integrates social learning and cognitive priming — media violence increases aggressive schemas, normative beliefs, and interpretation of ambiguous situations as hostile.

quizQuestions.question: Desensitization to media violence refers to:
options: Reduced emotional and physiological response to violent content with repeated exposure
Preference for non-violent media over time
quizQuestions.answer: Reduced emotional and physiological response to violent content with repeated exposure
quizQuestions.explanation: Repeated exposure to violent media reduces physiological arousal and empathic responding to violence through habituation.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Anderson and Bushman's General Aggression Model proposes that media violence increases aggression by:" is: Teaching aggression as a social norm, increasing aggressive thoughts, and priming hostile interpretations.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Teaching aggression as a social norm, increasing aggressive thoughts, and priming hostile interpretations is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Desensitization to media violence refers to:" is: Increased aggression after viewing violence.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Reduced emotional and physiological response to violent content with repeated exposure.

quizQuestions.question: Upward social comparison on social media most commonly leads to:
quizQuestions.answer: Decreased self-esteem and body satisfaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Comparing oneself to idealized social media profiles (upward comparison) consistently predicts lower self-esteem and body satisfaction.

quizQuestions.question: The 'highlight reel' phenomenon on social media refers to:
options: Users presenting curated, idealized versions of their lives rather than authentic experiences
quizQuestions.answer: Users presenting curated, idealized versions of their lives rather than authentic experiences
quizQuestions.explanation: Social media users typically share positive, peak experiences — creating highlight reels that lead to unfavorable social comparisons by viewers.

quizQuestions.question: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) is psychologically linked to:
options: Lower levels of social media use
Social comparison, anxiety, and reduced subjective well-being
quizQuestions.answer: Social comparison, anxiety, and reduced subjective well-being
quizQuestions.explanation: FOMO — awareness that others may be having rewarding experiences — is driven by social comparison and associated with anxiety and lower well-being.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Upward social comparison on social media most commonly leads to:" is: Decreased self-esteem and body satisfaction.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Decreased self-esteem and body satisfaction is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The Proteus Effect (Yee & Bailenson) found that avatar characteristics influence:
options: Only the avatar's performance in games
Player behavior — people behave in ways consistent with their avatar's appearance
quizQuestions.answer: Player behavior — people behave in ways consistent with their avatar's appearance
quizQuestions.explanation: The Proteus Effect: people adopt behaviors consistent with their avatar's visual appearance (attractive avatar → more confident behavior).

quizQuestions.question: Online disinhibition effect (Suler) refers to the tendency for people online to:
options: Be more polite than in person
Behave in ways they would not offline due to reduced social accountability
quizQuestions.answer: Behave in ways they would not offline due to reduced social accountability
quizQuestions.explanation: Online disinhibition — driven by anonymity, invisibility, asynchronicity, and dissociative imagination — leads to both more self-disclosure and more toxic behavior online.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Proteus Effect (Yee & Bailenson) found that avatar characteristics influence:" is: Player behavior — people behave in ways consistent with their avatar's appearance.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Player behavior — people behave in ways consistent with their avatar's appearance is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Variable ratio schedules in video games (random loot drops, rare item discoveries) are particularly effective at:
options: Reducing play time to healthy limits
Creating high, persistent rates of playing behavior resistant to extinction
quizQuestions.answer: Creating high, persistent rates of playing behavior resistant to extinction
quizQuestions.explanation: Variable ratio reinforcement — the most powerful schedule — explains why random rewards (loot boxes, rare drops) make gaming highly compelling and difficult to stop.

quizQuestions.question: Research on gaming and cognitive skills shows that action video games can improve:
options: Spatial reasoning, attention switching, and visual contrast sensitivity
quizQuestions.answer: Spatial reasoning, attention switching, and visual contrast sensitivity
quizQuestions.explanation: Controlled studies show action gaming improves specific cognitive skills including spatial reasoning, divided attention, and low-contrast visual perception.

quizQuestions.question: Virtual reality therapy for PTSD and phobias works primarily through:
options: Safe, controlled exposure to feared stimuli in immersive environments
quizQuestions.answer: Safe, controlled exposure to feared stimuli in immersive environments
quizQuestions.explanation: VR enables graded exposure therapy in immersive, controllable environments — effective for PTSD, phobias, and pain management.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Variable ratio schedules in video games (random loot drops, rare item discoveries) are particularly effective at:" is: Creating high, persistent rates of playing behavior resistant to extinction.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Creating high, persistent rates of playing behavior resistant to extinction is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Selective exposure to news media refers to:
options: Random exposure to various news sources
Deliberately choosing news sources that confirm existing beliefs
News reporters selecting which stories to cover
quizQuestions.answer: Deliberately choosing news sources that confirm existing beliefs
quizQuestions.explanation: Selective exposure is the tendency to consume media that aligns with preexisting attitudes, reinforcing confirmation bias.

quizQuestions.question: Filter bubbles (Pariser) are created when:
options: Users manually filter their news feeds
Algorithms personalize content based on past behavior, limiting exposure to diverse viewpoints
Social media users block accounts they disagree with
quizQuestions.answer: Algorithms personalize content based on past behavior, limiting exposure to diverse viewpoints
quizQuestions.explanation: Filter bubbles arise from algorithmic personalization that creates echo chambers, showing users more of what they already engage with.

quizQuestions.question: The illusory truth effect in misinformation research shows that:
options: False information is rejected after one exposure
Repeated exposure to false claims increases their perceived truth
People can always distinguish true from false news
Only factual news spreads rapidly online
quizQuestions.answer: Repeated exposure to false claims increases their perceived truth
quizQuestions.explanation: Illusory truth effect: mere repetition increases believability of statements — even false ones — due to processing fluency being misattributed to truth.

quizQuestions.question: Social comparison theory (Festinger) applied to media and body image predicts that:
options: Comparing oneself to media ideals always improves self-image
Upward comparisons to idealized media bodies reduce body satisfaction
Social comparison does not occur with media images
Downward comparisons are more common with media
quizQuestions.answer: Upward comparisons to idealized media bodies reduce body satisfaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Upward comparison to physically idealized media images consistently predicts lower body satisfaction and self-esteem, especially among women.

quizQuestions.question: Objectification theory (Fredrickson & Roberts) proposes that exposure to sexualized media images leads women to:
options: Habitual self-surveillance and evaluation of their own body as an observer
quizQuestions.answer: Habitual self-surveillance and evaluation of their own body as an observer
quizQuestions.explanation: Objectification theory holds that sexualized media treatment leads women to self-objectify — habitually monitoring their appearance from an outside observer's perspective.

quizQuestions.question: The thin-ideal internalization refers to:
options: Understanding that thin is a media construction
Accepting the culturally promoted thin body ideal as a personal goal
Rejecting unrealistic body standards promoted by media
quizQuestions.answer: Accepting the culturally promoted thin body ideal as a personal goal
quizQuestions.explanation: Thin-ideal internalization — adopting the cultural ideal of thinness as a personal standard — mediates the relationship between media exposure and disordered eating.

quizQuestions.question: Which population shows the strongest association between media body ideals and body dissatisfaction?
quizQuestions.answer: Adolescent girls and young women
quizQuestions.explanation: Research consistently shows adolescent girls and young women are most susceptible to body dissatisfaction from exposure to thin-ideal media images.

quizQuestions.question: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends that children aged 2–5 be limited to:
options: 1 hour per day of high-quality programming
3 hours per day of any content
quizQuestions.answer: 1 hour per day of high-quality programming
quizQuestions.explanation: The AAP recommends no more than 1 hour per day of high-quality media for children 2–5, with co-viewing by caregivers.

quizQuestions.question: Research on Sesame Street has shown that educational television for preschoolers can:
options: Improve vocabulary, letter knowledge, and school readiness
Replace the need for preschool education
quizQuestions.answer: Improve vocabulary, letter knowledge, and school readiness
quizQuestions.explanation: Longitudinal research on Sesame Street shows significant vocabulary, literacy, and school readiness benefits — a model for effective educational media.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends that children aged 2–5 be limited to:" is: 1 hour per day of high-quality programming.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. 1 hour per day of high-quality programming is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Research on Sesame Street has shown that educational television for preschoolers can:" is: Have no cognitive benefit.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Improve vocabulary, letter knowledge, and school readiness.

questions.question: Uses and Gratifications Theory positions media audiences as:
options: Active agents who select media to satisfy specific psychological needs
questions.answer: Active agents who select media to satisfy specific psychological needs
questions.explanation: Uses and Gratifications frames audiences as active, goal-directed media consumers — a key departure from passive audience models.

questions.question: Agenda-setting theory's 'second level' (attribute agenda-setting) refers to:
options: Which attributes or characteristics of issues and people media emphasize
The number of issues covered per broadcast
questions.answer: Which attributes or characteristics of issues and people media emphasize
questions.explanation: Second-level agenda-setting (McCombs et al.) holds that media not only tell us what to think about, but how to think about it — which attributes of issues are salient.

questions.question: The task-switching cost of media multitasking refers to:
options: Cognitive time and resource costs when shifting attention between tasks
The social cost of ignoring others while using media
Reduced physical activity from media use
questions.answer: Cognitive time and resource costs when shifting attention between tasks
questions.explanation: Task-switching produces a cognitive residue and time cost — attention is not seamlessly divided but alternated with switching costs each time.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The central route to persuasion (ELM) produces attitude change that is:" is: Enduring, resistant to counter-persuasion, and predictive of behavior.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Enduring, resistant to counter-persuasion, and predictive of behavior is correct.

questions.question: The General Aggression Model (Anderson & Bushman) proposes that media violence affects cognition by:
options: Increasing accessibility of aggressive thoughts and hostile attribution bias
Only affecting people with aggressive personality traits
questions.answer: Increasing accessibility of aggressive thoughts and hostile attribution bias
questions.explanation: Media violence primes aggressive concepts in memory and increases hostile attribution bias — interpreting ambiguous acts as aggressive.

questions.question: Which type of social media use is most strongly associated with reduced well-being?
questions.answer: Passive consumption (scrolling without interaction)
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows that passive scrolling is more strongly associated with envy, social comparison, and reduced well-being than active social interaction.

questions.question: The Proteus Effect demonstrates that avatar identity:
options: Influences real-world behavior through the avatar's visual characteristics
Only affects behavior within the game
questions.answer: Influences real-world behavior through the avatar's visual characteristics
questions.explanation: Yee & Bailenson's Proteus Effect: an attractive avatar leads to more confident behavior and a tall avatar leads to more assertive negotiation — even offline.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Loot boxes in video games are controversial because they:" is: Make games too easy.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Use gambling mechanics (random rewards for real money) that may target adolescents.

questions.question: Confirmation bias in news consumption means people tend to:
options: Seek balanced perspectives from multiple sources
Prefer and believe information that confirms their existing political views
Trust government sources over partisan sources
questions.answer: Prefer and believe information that confirms their existing political views
questions.explanation: Confirmation bias drives selective exposure to politically aligned news and preferential belief in attitude-consistent information.

questions.question: Objectification theory predicts that media portrayal of women's bodies as objects leads to:
options: Self-objectification, body shame, and impaired cognitive performance
Greater appreciation of diverse body types
questions.answer: Self-objectification, body shame, and impaired cognitive performance
questions.explanation: Fredrickson & Roberts found that self-objectification (triggered by objectifying media) occupies attention, produces shame, and impairs cognitive functioning (swimming suit study).

questions.question: The video deficit effect shows that infants under 2 learn best from:
options: Educational DVDs specifically designed for them
questions.answer: Live interactions with responsive humans
questions.explanation: Infants and toddlers show a significant learning deficit from video compared to identical live demonstrations — emphasizing the role of contingent social interaction.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Nomophobia most directly involves which emotion" is: Anxiety about smartphone separation.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Anxiety about smartphone separation is correct.

questions.question: Media literacy is best defined as:
options: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media across formats
Technical skill in using media devices
questions.answer: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media across formats
questions.explanation: Media literacy is the multidimensional set of competencies to critically consume, analyze, evaluate, and create media messages.

questions.question: Persuasive technology (Fogg) is controversial because it:
options: May exploit psychological vulnerabilities to change behavior without users' full awareness
Is always used for beneficial health goals
Requires explicit user consent to operate
questions.answer: May exploit psychological vulnerabilities to change behavior without users' full awareness
questions.explanation: Captology raises ethical concerns about designing technology to change behavior using psychological principles without transparent disclosure to users.

questions.question: The 'mean world syndrome' is most likely to develop in individuals who:
options: Watch large amounts of violent television programming
Use social media for 2 hours daily
questions.answer: Watch large amounts of violent television programming
questions.explanation: Gerbner's cultivation research specifically linked heavy dramatic television (with its high crime content) to mean world beliefs.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which theory best explains why people feel angry after watching political news that confirms their views" is: Cultivation theory.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Affective intelligence theory.

questions.question: Which media design feature most directly exploits the Zeigarnik effect to keep users engaged?
options: Autoplay of the next episode with a countdown timer
questions.answer: Autoplay of the next episode with a countdown timer
questions.explanation: Autoplay exploits the Zeigarnik effect — the story remains cognitively 'unfinished,' creating tension that the next episode promises to resolve.

questions.question: Research on advertising and children finds that children under approximately age 8:
options: Are better at identifying ads than adults
Cannot reliably distinguish advertising from programming or understand persuasive intent
Are less influenced by advertising than teenagers
questions.answer: Cannot reliably distinguish advertising from programming or understand persuasive intent
questions.explanation: Children under 8 lack the cognitive ability to recognize advertising's persuasive intent — making them particularly vulnerable to advertising effects.

questions.question: The frustration-aggression hypothesis applied to gaming predicts that online game aggression increases when:
options: Players lose or experience lag disrupting goal pursuit
questions.answer: Players lose or experience lag disrupting goal pursuit
questions.explanation: Goal frustration — from losing, lag, or unfair outcomes — predicts aggressive behavior (flaming, griefing) in online gaming contexts.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is NOT a key element of Bordin's working alliance framework applied to human-AI interaction research" is: Random assignment to conditions.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Random assignment to conditions is correct.

questions.question: The self-determination theory perspective on social media predicts that social media harms well-being when it:
options: Satisfies needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness
Replaces intrinsic motivation with external validation and surveillance
questions.answer: Replaces intrinsic motivation with external validation and surveillance
questions.explanation: SDT predicts that social media harms well-being when it undermines autonomous motivation — replacing intrinsic enjoyment with contingent self-worth from likes and followers.

questions.question: Deepfake technology poses the most direct threat to which psychological process?
options: Source monitoring and reality-monitoring — distinguishing authentic from fabricated information
questions.answer: Source monitoring and reality-monitoring — distinguishing authentic from fabricated information
questions.explanation: Deepfakes create highly realistic fabricated video/audio that challenges people's source monitoring — their ability to identify where information came from and whether it is real.

questions.question: Inoculation theory's implication for media literacy education is to:
options: Avoid exposing students to any misinformation
Pre-expose students to weakened misinformation techniques to build psychological resistance
Focus only on factual correction after exposure
Emphasize distrust of all media sources
questions.answer: Pre-expose students to weakened misinformation techniques to build psychological resistance
questions.explanation: Inoculation builds 'cognitive immunity' by pre-exposing students to misinformation techniques in a controlled educational context — making them resistant to future manipulation.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A key psychological benefit of parasocial relationships with media figures is:" is: Replacement of real relationships.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Providing sense of social belonging and connection for lonely individuals.

questions.question: Research on smartphone use before bed shows it most directly disrupts sleep through:
options: Blue light suppressing melatonin production and psychological arousal increasing alertness
questions.answer: Blue light suppressing melatonin production and psychological arousal increasing alertness
questions.explanation: Screen blue light suppresses melatonin (delaying sleep onset) while engaging content produces cognitive and emotional arousal — both disrupting sleep architecture.

questions.question: Body-positive media interventions have shown the greatest effectiveness in reducing:
questions.answer: Thin-ideal internalization and body dissatisfaction
questions.explanation: Controlled studies show body-positive interventions — diverse representations plus critical analysis — reduce thin-ideal internalization and body dissatisfaction.

questions.question: The concept of 'ethical design' in technology, informed by psychology, prioritizes:
options: User well-being, autonomy, and transparent persuasion over manipulation
questions.answer: User well-being, autonomy, and transparent persuasion over manipulation
questions.explanation: Ethical design frameworks (e.g., Center for Humane Technology) apply psychological principles to support well-being and autonomy rather than exploit vulnerabilities.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Research on VR therapy for social anxiety disorder has shown:" is: Promising efficacy equivalent to or approaching in-person exposure therapy.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Promising efficacy equivalent to or approaching in-person exposure therapy is correct.

questions.question: The psychological concept most relevant to understanding why misinformation corrective efforts often fail is:
options: The backfire effect (belief perseverance after disconfirmation)
questions.answer: The backfire effect (belief perseverance after disconfirmation)
questions.explanation: The backfire effect (Nyhan & Reifler) suggests that corrections can paradoxically strengthen beliefs in some cases — though more recent research shows this effect is not universal.

questions.question: The attention economy model implies that the most psychologically ethical media business model would be:
options: Maximizing engagement time to increase ad revenue
Subscription-based models that align company incentives with user well-being rather than attention maximization
Free platforms supported by targeted advertising
questions.answer: Subscription-based models that align company incentives with user well-being rather than attention maximization
questions.explanation: When platforms profit from attention time (ad model), they are incentivized to maximize engagement — often at the cost of user well-being. Subscription models align company success with user satisfaction.

questions.question: Thin-ideal internalization mediates the relationship between fashion media exposure and disordered eating, meaning it:
options: Directly causes disordered eating without psychological mediation
Explains the mechanism by which media exposure leads to eating disorder risk
Has no relationship to disordered eating
questions.answer: Explains the mechanism by which media exposure leads to eating disorder risk
questions.explanation: Thin-ideal internalization is the psychological mechanism through which exposure to idealized media bodies translates into body dissatisfaction and disordered eating risk.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which media psychology concept most directly explains why children who watch violent cartoons may not become violent adults" is: Catharsis hypothesis.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Developmental resilience and multiple mediating factors limiting effects.

questions.question: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) pose unique psychological challenges primarily related to:
options: Identity, agency, privacy, and the boundary between self and technology
questions.answer: Identity, agency, privacy, and the boundary between self and technology
questions.explanation: BCIs raise profound psychological questions: what constitutes authentic self-expression when cognition is mediated by technology, who owns neural data, and how agency is experienced.

questions.question: Which finding from media psychology research has had the most significant positive policy impact?
options: Cultivation theory's influence on TV content rating systems
Agenda-setting theory's influence on newspaper front pages
The Proteus Effect in avatar design
Uses and gratifications research on radio listenership
questions.answer: Cultivation theory's influence on TV content rating systems
questions.explanation: Cultivation research and related violence effects work directly influenced the development of content rating systems (TV-PG, TV-MA) and watershed content policies.

questions.question: A psychologically healthy relationship with media and technology is best characterized by:
options: Mindful, intentional use that supports goals and well-being without displacement of real-world relationships or functioning
Using media only for information gathering
questions.answer: Mindful, intentional use that supports goals and well-being without displacement of real-world relationships or functioning
questions.explanation: Psychologically healthy media use is intentional and balanced — supporting goals, maintaining real-world functioning, and not displacing higher-quality activities or relationships.
